turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court this morning in Buffkin versus Collins.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. Congress requires the Department of Veteran Affairs to apply the "benefit of the doubt rule" when resolving veterans' claims for service-related disability benefits. That rule requires the VA to give the veteran the benefit of the doubt on any material issue when the evidence for and against the veteran is in approximate balance. The VA's benefits determinations are reviewable by the US Court of Appeals for veterans' claims, which we call the Veterans Court. Congress has specified that when reviewing VA decisions, the Veterans Court must review legal questions de novo and factual questions for clear error. In 2002, Congress enacted a new provision, which requires the Veterans Court to take due account of the VA's application of the benefit of the doubt rule when reviewing VA decisions. Petitioners are veterans who are dissatisfied with the VA's resolution of their claims. They argued before the Federal Circuit that the provision commanding the Veterans Court to take due account requires the Veterans Court to review the VA's application of the benefit of the doubt rule de novo. The Federal Circuit rejected that argument holding that the VA's application of the benefit of the doubt rule is a factual determination reviewed only for clear error. In an opinion filed with the Clerk today, we affirm. We hold that the statutory command to take due account requires the Veterans Court to review the VA's application of the benefit of the doubt rule under the standards that Congress set forth in the statute defining the scope of the Veterans Court's Review. And because the VA's approximate balance determination is a predominantly factual decision, we hold that the Veterans Court must review it for clear error. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Federal Court. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Gorsuch joins.
